data.title: Erasca: Strong Data Overshadowed By A Patient Death And A Patent Fight
data.description:
Overview

Erasca (ERAS) is a perfect example of both the alpha and volatility that are inherent in biotechnology investing. Its stock was trading in the low single digits to begin the year before reaching 52-week highs of $24 per share.

The gains were attributed to RAS hype and early encouraging signals.

Erasca is a clinical-stage precision oncology biotech that is focused on RAS/MAPK pathway-driven cancers. Its lead drug candidate, ERAS-0015, is described as “an oral, highly potent pan-RAS molecular glue.” It is designed to improve upon mutant-selective inhibitors like Lumakras to overcome the resistance that can result from narrower inhibition.

This has broad implications in oncology because RAS mutations drive nearly one-fourth of all solid tumors. Think non-small cell lung cancer, pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, and colorectal cancer.

Now, there was a big development yesterday, and this is why Erasca’s stock is down over 40% in intraday trading at writing. The company dropped preliminary Phase 1 dose escalation data in KRAS G12X mutant solid tumors. Despite encouraging efficacy data, the stock appears down for multiple reasons.

In the article below, I talk about those reasons and try to get to the bigger picture to see if the stock merits speculation ahead of the next meaningful data reveal in the first half of 2027.

Reasons for Weakness

There was a safety signal disclosed around the data release and call that may have triggered sell-off fears. Now, the company went out of its way to put the patient's death into context.

The Grade 5 (death) pneumonitis case surrounded a 66-year-old heavily pre-treated pancreatic cancer patient who developed Grade 3 pneumonitis on 24 mg. The patient then elected to withdraw supportive care and progressed to death. Management called this a “rare event.”

Now, this is a class effect. In other words, pneumonitis is a known rare risk factor when you are on a G12C inhibitor (i.e., Lumakras, Krazati). Both labels carry official “Warnings and Precautions” for Interstitial Lung Disease and pneumonitis.

Moreover, investigators believed had this patient continued care, there may have been a different outcome. And importantly, there were no other Grade 4 or 5 events.

So, investors may have been more focused on this. And then on top of that, its competitor, Revolution Medicines (RVMD), sent a letter alleging that ERAS-0015 infringes upon their U.S. Patent No. 12,409,225.

While the company claims that this is “without merit,” it adds near-term legal uncertainty and risk to its lead asset.

Finally, I think this was just a classic case of “sell the news.” We have smaller cohorts and early unconfirmed data that can easily lead to some skepticism afterwards amid a $5 billion-plus valuation.

Reasons for Optimism

Phase 1 is not just for safety. It also looks at pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics.

Regarding the drug's PK profile, it looks well-behaved, with linear, dose-proportional exposure up to the max administered dose, which is 40 mg daily.

Regarding PD and biomarkers, it saw strong on-target activity. Between 16 and 32 mg daily, 100% of the 14 evaluable patients had an equal to or greater than 75% reduction in KRAS G12X ctDNA variant allele fraction, and 5 of those 14 patients had complete clearance.

Turning to efficacy in second-line plus non-small cell lung cancer, it saw a 62% uORR (u = unconfirmed) at 8 weeks (75% in post-ICI/platinum patients).

Upon a cross-trial comparison (limitations aside), this data exceeded Revolution Medicines’ RMC-6236 (daraxonrasib) by as much as 37 percentage points per management.

Of course, I always caution readers to be wary of drawing conclusions from cross-trial comparisons.

In second-line pancreatic cancer, the uORR was between 40 and 50%. As is typical in this indication, tumor shrinkage can be seen early, but RECIST responses can be delayed.

And in colorectal cancer, the first patient on 16 mg plus standard Vectibix had a 34% shrinkage at the first scan.

What's most impressive and perhaps key is that almost every responder, lung and pancreatic, remains on treatment, which is a good sign for durability, tolerability, and safety.

The Middle Ground

Perhaps if the expectations weren't so high to begin with, the market would be more welcoming of the data. The monotherapy data are clearly de-risked enough to expand, and ERAS-0015 has potential in combinations.

At a glance, the data do look differentiated. The ORR is higher, and tolerability looks cleaner. So I think the “molecular glue plus superior PK” story is legit. And looking ahead, the next big data readout is slated for the first half of next year. Management expects select mono expansion and combo dose escalation data. So, this may be a test of durability and combo safety. It may also tell us whether or not the pneumonitis event was a "one-off." I do suspect that if all of those responses confirm, prove durable, and the combos stay clean, Erasca's thesis would meaningfully strengthen.

Developments regarding ERAS-0015 are particularly important because their other main asset, ERAS-4001, which is a pan-KRAS inhibitor, is in earlier development, with preliminary Phase 1 data expected later this year.

Put the headphones on and remove all the noise, and you're left with a high-risk, high-reward biotech with binary data events, competition, IP overhang, and execution risk. So while the post-crash valuation is more reasonable, given the stage, volatility should persist.

Financial Health

Erasca ended 2025 with $342 million in cash and marketable securities. This figure does not include the $259 million raised from a January offering. Erasca's pro forma cash and marketable securities were $434 million as of the March update. The company believes its cash runway will extend into the second half of 2028.

While it varies, in the previous four years, the company has burned roughly $100 million annually in cash from operations, so management's cash runway guidance is consistent and probably also considers higher costs associated with expanding their assets into later-stage trials.

Conclusion

Given recent developments, I feel pretty comfortable recommending a “Hold” rating on ERAS. I can really see the case for speculation and caution. It's clear that hopes were high going into Phase 1 data. To me, preliminary Phase 1 data de-risks the program. The response rates are favorable to its competitors on a cross-trial basis, and the tolerability profile looks pretty clean. Of course, the single Grade 5 pneumonitis event is getting a lot of attention, but it doesn't appear all that surprising given the known class effects.

Still, I've seen this story play out many times, and these biotech valuations can reach several billion dollars on Phase 1 hope. And then it turns out that the drug isn't safe or isn't differentiated enough, and the stock falls in excess of 90%. So, this is a stock that needs to be evaluated in the context of a barbell portfolio.

data.title: Celestica Suffers From Outsized Growth Prospects And Momentum Fatigue
data.description:
I previously rated Celestica Inc. (CLS) as a Hold in January 2026, attributed to their premium valuations beyond their Original Design Manufacturer (ODM) peers.

In this article, I shall discuss why CLS remains a Hold here, despite the robust profitable growth prospects from the multi-year cloud super cycle.

CLS Proves Its AI Beneficiary Status

Since my last Hold rating, my retracement conjecture nearer to the 200-day moving averages of $218s has not materialized, as observed in CLS being well defended along the Q4 '25/Q1 '26 trading ranges of $250s-$270s.

This is before the wider market bottoms by the end of March 2026, and many AI-related stocks are buoyed to new highs after the overly done pessimism in late 2025.

This is significantly aided by the Q1 '26 earnings season also reiterating the durability of the multi-year cloud spending trends.

1. Multi-Year Hyperscaler Spending Trends

CLS has benefitted from the hyperscalers' outsized capex guidance at approximately $700B in 2026, "nearly six times 2022 levels," thanks to three core customers representing 65% of its revenues in FQ1 '26 (+14 points YoY), likely including Alphabet/Google (GOOG) and Meta (META), page 3.

With GOOG already guiding FY2026 capex of $180B at the midpoint (+96.8% YoY) and META at $165.5B (+129.1% YoY), it is unsurprising that CLS has been well buoyed in the recent April 2026 rally.

This is significantly aided by the recently announced partnership with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), likely to diversify CLS' customer concentration risks.

These may have directly contributed to the management:

  • raising their FY2026 revenue guidance to $19B (+53.3% YoY) and adj EPS to $10.15 (+67.7% YoY), compared to the prior guidance of +37.2% YoY and +44.6% YoY, respectively,
  • hinting at further outperformance in 2027, as "we now expect to grow revenue significantly more than this in 2027, as a result of improved visibility and new program wins," and
  • hinting at their elongated "booking awards right now that ship into 2028 (FQ1 '26 earnings transcript)" with favorable and non-cancelable, non-returnable contractual terms.

These developments are not surprising indeed, given the robust demand for GOOG's TPU server offerings, with Anthropic (ANTHRO) already signing multiple gigawatts of the next-generation TPU capacity for 2027, as with META.

These naturally lend great credibility to CLS' promising multi-year guidance, aided by their intensified R&D efforts by +134% YoY and the ongoing capacity expansions allowing them to win multi-generation hyperscaler contracts.

This is especially since the company is ramping up across numerous 400G/800G ethernet programs in FQ1'26, and an expansion to the 1.6T ethernet programs is expected in H2 '26, with it building upon their existing rack program across liquid cooling/advanced scale infrastructure/thermal management/networking.

The expanding customer/partnership base and the growing hyperscaler programs naturally underscore CLS' confidence in guiding the high double-digit growth prospects while contributing to the stock's outsized rally thus far.

2. FQ1 '26 Performance Discussed

For now, CLS' growth prospects are tied to their Connectivity & Cloud Solutions (CCS) segment, with the robust hyperscaler spending trends already contributing to:

  • the accelerating CCS revenue growth to $3.24B in FQ1 '26 (+76% YoY) against a year ago at +28% YoY and
  • the richer profit margins of 8.6% (+0.6 points YoY/+6.1 from FY2019 levels of 2.5%) against a year ago at +1.2 points YoY.

Therefore, while the Advanced Technology Solutions (ATS) segment across the Aerospace and Defense/Industrial/HealthTech end markets has reported flattish performance on a YoY basis, it is unsurprising that CLS has been able to deliver the notably richer performance metrics in FQ1 '26 across:

  • the overall revenues of $4.05B (+52.8% YoY),
  • the adj operating margins of 8% (+0.9 points YoY/+5.3 from FY2019 levels of 2.7%),
  • the adj EPS of $2.16 (+80% YoY), and
  • the adj Return On Invested Capital (ROIC) of 49.8% (+18.3 points YoY/+40.6 from FY2019 levels of 9.2%),

as their bottom lines also benefit from the improved manufacturing/operating leverage and the ongoing share repurchases cancelling the elevated SBC expenses, despite the single-digit Original Design Manufacturer (ODM) profit margins.

3. Profitable Growth Trends Overly Priced In

Thanks to the FQ1 '26 outperformance and the promising FY2026/FY2027 guidance, it is unsurprising that the consensus has raised their forward estimates, with CLS expected to record top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +36.5%/+41.2% through FY2028.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +16.6%/+43.9% and a 10Y CAGR of +8.2%/+20.7%, respectively, with the accelerated growth prospects naturally exemplifying their AI beneficiary status.

On the other hand, despite the raised consensus forward estimates somewhat balancing the outsized stock price rally, it goes without saying that CLS continues to trade at relatively premium FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 46.88x.

This is up from the 1Y mean of 34.21x, the 5Y mean of 16.59x, and the sector median of 23.86x.

Based on the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +41.2%, the stock may first appear to be trading reasonably at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.13x, compared to the sector median of 1.48x.

Even so, when comparing CLS' FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 46.88x and my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.13x to its direct AI infrastructure ODM peers, including:

  • supplier for CoreWeave (CRWV), Oracle (ORCL), and xAI - Super Micro Computer (SMCI) at 12.37x/0.67x (using the same methodology),
  • supplier for Microsoft (MSFT) and ORCL - Hon Hai Precision Industry (HNHPF) (2317:TW) at 13.39x/0.70x,
  • supplier for META and Amazon AWS (AMZN) - Quanta Computer (QUCCF) at 14.06x/0.88x, and
  • supplier for AMZN AWS - Jabil (JBL) at 27.59x/1.43x, respectively,

it is apparent that CLS is trading at a relative premium to its ODM peers, with the recent rally overly done.

As an ODM, CLS' high-growth prospects are also directly linked to their ability to ramp up their manufacturing capacity fast enough to meet the growing demand, as similarly observed in the outsized capex of $218.4M in FQ1'26 (+495% YoY).

Readers must note that a similar trend has been observed in other AI-related infrastructure players, most significantly, with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), albeit with both CLS and TSM still generating robust Free Cash Flows and healthy balance sheets, allowing them to continue investing in their capacity expansions.

Nonetheless, it goes without saying that CLS' premium FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 46.88x and my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.13x have been overly done.

This is especially when compared to the world's largest foundry by market share, TSM at 26.21x/0.79x, respectively, with it lending credence to my caution at CLS' current inflated levels.

CLS Remains A Hold Here

For now, CLS has already broken out of the Q4 '25/Q1 '26 sideways trading before charting a new high of $423s at the time of writing, with it remaining to be seen how long the upward momentum may last.

Thanks to the +69.2% stock price rally from the March 2026 bottom, it is unsurprising that CLS has also entered overbought territories as observed in the inflated RSIs, with a momentum reversal and/or sideways trading cadence likely to occur at the current inflated levels.

This is especially since the stock has "tumbled" post-FQ1 '26 earnings call despite the robust performance metrics, with the momentum fatigue implying that most of the growth prospects are already baked into its stock prices/valuations.

Pending further catalysts, I believe that CLS is at best likely to trade sideways between the new highs of $420s and the Q4 '25/Q1 '26 resistance levels of $350s in the intermediate term, similar to those observed in Q4 '25/Q1 '26, as the stock slowly grows into its premium valuations.

My caution is attributed to the stock already pulling forward much of its upside potential to:

  • my base-case long-term price target of $406.50, based on the sector P/E median of 23.86x and the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $17.04, and
  • it remains to be seen if my bull-case (and speculative) long-term price target of $798.80 may materialize, based on the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 46.88x and the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $17.04.

With the FQ1 '26 earnings call behind us, I am of the opinion that CLS' return prospects are likely to be underwhelming at current inflated levels, resulting in my reiterated Hold rating.

Interested investors are better off waiting for a steep correction to occur, preferably nearer to the potential technical support levels of $350s, with those levels also nearer to my bull-case fair value estimates of $329 (based on the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 46.88x and the LTM adj EPS performance of $7.02).

Given the potential -19.5% downside at the midpoint from current levels, I urge some patience in the meantime.

data.title: Doximity: Buy It As Mr. Market Is Selling It Cheap Under A Panic Attack
data.description:
Introduction

This article endeavors to explore to what extent the stock price decline from $65 to $25, about two-thirds of its value, of Doximity, Inc. (DOCS) during the last six months could be justified by the deterioration of its business fundamentals, and to what extent such decline results from Mr. Market’s mood swing.

After going over the business model of the company, reviewing its last eight years' growth trajectories, checking its balance sheets, and reading the last six months’ news releases, I conclude that Mr. Market has suffered from a case of panic attack and has consequently been selling DOCS shares on the cheap out of the fears inspired by the prevailing but unjustified negative sentiments against the enterprise software industry. Such fears have been compounded by the short-term uncertainties surrounding the marketing budgets of Doximity's pharma and health care customers.

Therefore, it is a good time to acquire shares in Doximity, a reasonably priced stock supported by a decent moat, a steady growth trajectory, a superb profitability profile, and a clean balance sheet.

The Business Model of Doximity

The first line of its last Form 10-K, aka Annual Report, describes its business as follows:

We are the leading digital platform for U.S. medical professionals, with over two million registered members as of March 31, 2025. Our members include more than 80% of U.S. physicians… along with over 60% of U.S. nurse practitioners and physician assistants, and over 90% of graduating U.S. medical students.

It is said Doximity is the LinkedIn for healthcare professionals. However, it is more than just a depot for their resumes. The 10-K continues:

Our "Workflow Solutions,” which include our telehealth, on-call scheduling, and AI-powered workflow tools, are designed to help clinicians streamline their clinical workflow, reduce their administrative burden, and easily connect with patients and colleagues.

If you have heard doctors complaining about the time they have spent on doing paperwork, and fighting insurance companies (and a few rotten apples from my profession), "Workflow Solutions" sounds like enterprise software for them to make their lives easier.

Talking about my profession, I run a small law firm and truly wish that someone would create a Doximity equivalent for us lawyers so I could connect with my law school classmates and Big Law alums and automate the client intake, time entries, billing, and related office tasks of my law partners. Better yet, the platform and workflow solutions (it had better be AI-powered) should be free to us, just like Doximity is for healthcare professionals!

Now you may ask, “But how does Doximity make money then?” The 10-K tells you all:

Our “Marketing Solutions” enable our pharmaceutical and health system customers to get the right content, services, and peer connections to the right medical professionals through a variety of modules ... We count the top pharmaceutical manufacturers, hospitals, and health systems as our customers.

Now you see Doximity is the gatekeeper (read: toll collector) for pharmaceutical companies to promote their medicines and medical devices to healthcare professionals and for hospitals to recruit them. Too bad nobody wants to promote anything through us lawyers!

Hey, Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly, want to bid on the prime GLP-1 advertising board with us?

Doximity's Revenue and Profits of the Last 8 Years

Below is a table that I have compiled using the income statement data of the company from Seeking Alpha. From there, you can see the revenue and gross profit of Doximity have grown by 7.5-fold, and the operating income by 35-fold. While the revenue growth has slowed down from its early years of over 50% YOY, it still commands a respectable growth rate in the mid-teens.

Partially offset by the increase in the number of outstanding shares by 3-fold, the EPS of the company has climbed from a range between $0.01 and $0.23 during the first 3 years to a range between $0.53 and $0.72 during the second 3 years, and now arrives at roughly $1.10 during the last fiscal year and TTM.

As measured by the nearly 90% gross profit margin and now steadily above 30% net profit margins, this is a highly profitable business. The reason is simple: it essentially operates a toll booth with the construction of the road and related infrastructure substantially completed already. It does not need to perform the necessary maintenance and update work, such as implementing AI-empowered functions, such as note-taking during patient virtual or in-person meetings, but it has acquired its key assets, i.e., the network of the majority of U.S. healthcare professionals.

The Clean Balance Sheet

The company operates an asset-light business. So it is not a surprise that one will find over $700 million in cash and market securities, $156 million of accounts receivable, and $100 million of prepaid expenses, but only $18 million of equipment (I suppose some servers) in its latest Form 10-Q, aka Quarterly Report. It has only $84 million of goodwill, meaning it has not done a lot of acquisitions and has grown its business organically.

On the liability side, it has only $177 million in total liabilities.

In short, even if the business stops growing or becomes much less profitable than it is now, the downside is very limited. It will be extremely unlikely for the company to run into any liquidity issues.

So Why Did the Stock Lose 2/3 of Its Value During the Last Six Months?

Fiscal Q2 of 2026

In November 2025 (earnings release summarized by Seeking Alpha), Doximity delivered a standout fiscal Q2/2026 earnings release. Its quarterly revenue of $168.5 million was 23% higher YOY, beating expectations by a wide margin. Profitability remained exceptional, with adjusted EBITDA margins around 60% and free cash flow rising sharply to $91.6 million. Operationally, engagement hit record levels across its newsfeed, workflow tools, and AI offerings, with usage growing over 50% sequentially.

However, the stock declined ~$65 down to ~$52 immediately after the Q2 earnings release despite good reported results, ostensibly because management alluded to the uncertainty in its pharmaceutical clients' market budgets due to healthcare policy changes. Since Doximity’s revenue is closely tied to pharma marketing budgets, even a temporary delay or hesitation in spending raised concerns about near-term revenue visibility.

First off, this sounds more like a short-term challenge than a fundamental deterioration of the business prospects of Doximity. Second, it is rather vague to me. I have struggled to figure out what it specifically means, but to no avail after days of research. Has there been any proposed legislation restricting American pharmaceutical companies from trying to promote their products and services to healthcare professionals? I could not find anything one way or another.

Meanwhile, the stock continued its slide from ~$52 to ~$35 before the Q3/2026 earnings release in early February 2026.

Readers are welcome to share in the Comments section below as to what they believe may have caused the dramatic stock price in such a short time period.

Fiscal Q3 of 2026

In fiscal Q3/2026 (the earnings release summarized by Seeking Alpha), Doximity maintained solid but decelerating growth. Revenue reached $185.1 million, up 10% year-over-year, marking a clear slowdown from Q2’s pace. Adjusted EBITDA remained strong at $111.4 million with margins above 60%, but GAAP earnings and free cash flow declined year-over-year, reflecting higher investment levels, particularly in AI infrastructure. Despite this, the business continued to exhibit strong unit economics, with gross margins still above 90% and robust cash generation. From an operational standpoint, Workflow Solutions usage climbed to a record 720,000 prescribers, and AI tools reached over 300,000 users.

However, management made clear that AI products are still largely unmonetized outside hospital systems, meaning that while they enhance engagement, they are not yet contributing materially to revenue growth.

After Q3/2026, the stock price declined from ~ $35 to ~ $27, ostensibly driven more directly by disappointing guidance and a weakening growth narrative. Management guided Q4 revenue well below consensus expectations, reinforcing the perception of slowing growth.

At the same time, investors became more skeptical about the timing of AI monetization: while AI usage was growing rapidly, it was increasing costs without contributing to revenue. Combined with lingering client budget uncertainty, this created a mismatch between strong current metrics and deteriorating forward expectations—precisely the kind of setup that leads to multiple compressions.

The Stock Is Reasonably Valued Now

There is little doubt that the stock is no longer as highly valued as it used to be, especially as compared to when it was traded for ~$100 in 2021. However, the stock now is undervalued relative to its peers in the healthcare sector (is it really a healthcare company that has to live with the curse of patent cliffs?) as measured by GAAP or non-GAAP earnings or EBIT or EBITDA or cash flow. To the extent it is more highly valued relative to its sales, one may recall that its gross profit of ~90% and net profit margins of ~30% are substantially higher than the average healthcare sector company, and a lot of companies in the sector do not have any revenue, let alone gross profit or net profit to speak of.

Risks to a Bullish Thesis

The key risks to my bullish thesis are the following:

First, since Doximity has reached the majority of U.S. healthcare professionals, it may not have further space to grow. Considering that promoting medicines and medical devices through healthcare professionals may be a somewhat American thing, there is limited potential for Doximity to expand overseas. This apparent risk may also be a blessing in disguise, as it could mean that Doximity is unlikely to have a large competitor that tries to get into this business and take its market share from it.

Second, a better platform may emerge, especially given AI's power, to slowly or even rapidly replace Doximity's role in automating the work flow of healthcare professionals and the operations of small medical offices and large hospitals alike.

Third, there may be policy changes on how medicines and medical devices may be marketed in the U.S. I believe that is unlikely and difficult to implement in a society that believes in free enterprise, and marketing to healthcare professionals is essentially a constitutional right, i.e., the freedom of speech for commercial enterprises that the government cannot severely restrict.

Conclusion

Having not found any specific and convincing factors leading to the decline by two-thirds of the stock price of DOCS, especially the decline between ~$52 and ~$35 when there were no substantive developments or earnings releases, I believe that Mr. Market has suffered from a case of a short-term-focused panic attack and has consequently been selling DOCS shares out of fears inspired by the prevailing sentiments against the enterprise software industry.

Doximity makes its profit as a tollbooth, but it probably has been understood and perceived to be an enterprise software provider to hospitals and medical offices alike. The prevailing fear that AI would replace traditional software, including enterprise software, has probably affected Doximity, partly causing its stock price to decline from ~$65 to ~$25 in a matter of six months without any deterioration of its business fundamentals to a commensurate extent.

This hypothesis is supported by the fact that during the same time window, the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) has declined from ~115 to ~85.

Such fears have been compounded by the less-than-vague short-term uncertainties surrounding the marketing budgets of Doximity's pharma and health care customers.

However, if we zoom out of the risks related to the short-term or cyclical fluctuation of pharma marketing budgets, we will see in DOCS a reasonably priced stock supported by a steadily growing and highly profitable business with little debt and a decent moat.

For the above reasons, I rate DOCS a cautious BUY and will build up a position here while keeping an eye on its business performance.

data.title: Aktis Oncology: 'Buy' Based On Radiotherapeutic Isotope Design For Broad Targeting
data.description:
I'm initiating coverage of Aktis Oncology (AKTS) with a "Buy" rating because this radiopharmaceutical biotech holds a lot of potential. That's because this is not just another radiopharmaceutical company; it is looking to use its miniprotein radioconjugates AKY-1189 and AKY-2519 to target specific types of solid tumors. AKY-1189 is being developed to target Nectin-4-expressing solid tumors in an ongoing phase 1b study. The reason why I believe this program is significant, plus why I will be going over it below in detail, is because the company expects to release Part 1 data from the ongoing phase 1b study in Q1 of 2027. Then, the company is developing another miniprotein radioconjugate as part of its pipeline; this would be AKY-2519, which is being developed to target patients with B7-H3-expressing solid tumors. With respect to this candidate, the company intends to present two poster presentations at the upcoming American Society of Clinical Oncology [ASCO] medical conference on May 30, 2026.

The beauty of this program is that the FDA cleared an IND for AKY-2519 in March of 2026, and the company expects to initiate a phase 1b study for this program targeting B7-H3-expressing solid tumors in mid-2026. The beauty of this company is evolving the landscape of wanting to expand the radiopharmaceutical market with these two candidates. The reason why is current radiopharmaceuticals of PSMA and SSTR2 only go after a small subset of patients. By moving forward towards the other targets [Nectin-4 and B7-H3], it hopes to increase the addressable market and bring its technology to the next level. The miniprotein radioconjugates that the company is developing are established to deliver the alpha-emitting radioisotope actinium-225 to solid tumors expressing the two protein targets I just mentioned.

The thing is that this biotech is well equipped because of the way it has engineered its radioconjugates AKY-1189 and AKY-2519, which are to deeply target tumor tissue but to be cleared rapidly from healthy tissue. With the upcoming ASCO 2026 poster presentations of AKY-2519 targeting patients with B7-H3-expressing solid tumors, plus the expected data from Part 1 of a phase 1b study using AKY-1189 for the treatment of patients with Nectin-4-expressing solid tumors, I believe that I should start off this company with a "Buy" rating. This is because radiopharmaceutical therapies are proven, but the key difference is that Aktis is looking to develop its drug with alpha-emitting isotopes. Along with the fact that it has plenty of cash runway to 2029, especially since it just completed its initial public offering [IPO]. Lastly, it has a partnership with Eli Lilly (LLY).

Miniprotein Radioconjugates In Place To Potentially Generate Shareholder Value

As I mentioned above, Aktis Oncology is looking to enter the radiotherapeutic development space. However, it is not looking to be a "me too" type of radiotherapeutic development company. Instead, it is looking to change the scope of this class of therapeutics by being able to enhance what can be targeted thus far. The second candidate, AKY-2519, which is being developed to target solid tumor patients with B7-H3-expressing solid tumors, is not expected to have a phase 1b study initiated until mid-2026. This provides another key opportunity for this biotech. However, for the purpose of this article, I'm specifically going to go over the development of its miniprotein radioconjugate AKY-1189 for the treatment of patients with Nectin-4-expressing solid tumors in Part 1 of a phase 1b study. Before going over this clinical trial in detail, plus any catalysts to be released from this program, I believe that it is first important to understand what types of solid tumors can be targeted. First and foremost, there are several solid tumor types that have this expression, and they are:

  • Locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma [mUC] - 2nd line targeting of patients with 86% expression of Nectin-4
  • Breast cancer - 2nd line + targeting of patients with 83% Nectin-4 expression
  • Cervical cancer - 2nd line + 82% Nectin-4 expression
  • Non-small cell lung cancer - 2nd line + 72% Nectin 4 expression

The company notes a huge market opportunity for both of its radiotherapeutics in development, plus additional ones to come into the pipeline. The radiopharmaceuticals market is projected to reach between $25 and $60 billion post-2030 [Aktis Oncology Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #7]. There are two reasons to mention here immediately. The first of which is that its technology improving the therapeutic window is one key to unlocking such a large market. The second item is what I mentioned above: undruggable targets not yet tapped with other radiopharmaceuticals.

It is important to note that this company intends to explore all the solid tumor targets that have high Nectin-4 expression. However, the one indication I want to go over is the targeting of locally advanced or metastatic urothelial cancer [mUC] patients who have a high expression of this particular protein. This makes sense as a good target because, from what I gather, it could be the highest expression of Nectin-4 in solid tumors. This is based on the demographic the company has provided. There is another reason to mention this particular indication, which is that the company has already received FDA Fast Track Designation [FTD] of AKY-1189 for the treatment of these locally advanced or metastatic mUC patients. Why was this done so? That's because it fills an unmet medical need. Specifically, the designation was given so that this miniprotein radioconjugate could be given to these adults with mUC who have progressed on or after having received systemic therapies.

The point here is that once these patients receive PADCEV, their options are severely limited. The hope here is that AKY-1189 could potentially help to offer these patients new hope. There is a good chance that the company can do better compared to currently available therapies. For instance, PADCEV is approved to treat mUC. It is a strong antibody drug conjugate [ADC] that is expected to reach worldwide peak sales of $7 billion [Aktis Oncology Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #8]. If that isn't enough, then you can take a look at a radioligand therapeutic [RLT] PLUVICTO, which has been approved for PSMA-positive metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer [PSMA-positive mCRPC]. Peak worldwide sales of this RLT are expected to be $5.4 billion. The point is that this company is able to target a larger pool of patients because of going after several Nectin-4-expressing/B7-H3-expressing solid tumors using AKY-1189 and AKY-2519, respectively.

The mechanism of action [MOA] for AKY-1189 as a miniprotein radioconjugate is to selectively deliver the alpha-emitting radioisotope actinium-225 to target solid tumors. In the case of this candidate, the goal is to only go after Nectin-4-expressing solid tumors. The beauty of these miniproteins is the ability to allow their radiotherapeutics to go after broad targets in solid tumor targeting. This is because of several advantages in place, like being able to achieve deep tumor penetration, but at the same time, being cleared immediately from healthy tissue of the body. The point is to offer an improved molecule as a radiotherapeutic with a broader reach beyond that of only targeting PSMA and/or only SSTR2 [Aktis Oncology Corporate Presentation March 2026, slide #5]. This means the ability to expand the scope of this technology to effectively treat more tumor types with it.

To see whether or not Aktis Oncology will be able to effectively treat Nectin-4-expressing solid tumor patients, it is in the process of evaluating Part 1 of the phase 1b open-label study targeting patients with locally advanced or metastatic solid tumors. It is important to state that Part 1 is only the dose-escalation portion of this early-stage trial. The goal? The point of this portion is to establish two items, which are:

  • Maximum tolerated dose [MTD]
  • Recommended phase 2 dose [RP2D]

Part 2 is the dose-expansion portion of this phase 1b trial, which is intended to take the RP2D from Part 1 and then expand it to three cohorts to be deployed for it. This specific program brings about a major catalyst opportunity for shareholders to look forward to. It is expected that Aktis Oncology will release preliminary results from this phase 1b study using AKY-1189 to treat locally advanced or metastatic Nectin-4-expressing solid tumor patients in Q1 of 2027. Isn't this catalyst quite a bit of time far out? It is expected in the early part of 2027, but there might be other milestones to keep an eye on during 2026. The first item is that of what I mentioned at the beginning above, which is that of imaging and dosimetry data of AKY-2519 targeting B7-H3-expressing solid tumor patients to be presented as two poster presentations at ASCO 2026. Secondly, there is an expectation in mid-2026 that the company will initiate a phase 1b study using AKY-2519 to target this specific group of solid tumor patients.

Financials

The truth is that the financial situation for this company is highly sound based on its 10-K SEC filing. There are two things to note here. The first of which is that this company has provided that as of December 31, 2025, it ended with cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $226.8 million. This is a good amount of cash, of course, but what's even better is that it announced the pricing of an upsized IPO in January of 2026. This is the second important item to mention relating to its financials. Specifically, it was able to raise $333.5 million from its IPO and add to its cash ending December 31, 2025. Adding both of these together brings about a pro forma cash position of $562.1 million. Having said this, in my opinion, I believe that there is no risk of near-term dilution for this company. Why is that? The reason why I state this is because the company believes that its pro forma cash ending December 31, 2025, of $562.1 million will allow it to fund its operations into 2029.

Risks to Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Aktis Oncology. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing development of miniprotein radioconjugate AKY-1189 for the targeting of patients with locally advanced or metastatic Nectin-4-expressing solid tumors in Part 1 of the ongoing phase 1b study. The company is expected to release preliminary data from this part of this early-stage trial in Q1 of 2027. The risk here is that even though other RLTs like PLUVICTO have been shown to work well in treating patients specifically with PSMA-positive mCRPC patients, there is no assurance that AKY-1189 will achieve a similar or superior level of success as a differentiated RLT. Especially since AKY-1189 deploys an alpha-emitting isotope, actinium-225, and PLUVICTO uses a beta-emitting isotope, lutetium-177. The thing is that an alpha-emitting isotope carries the advantage of a stronger break of DNA damage [efficacy], but on the flip side, it has a shorter range. Whereas PLUVICTO has a longer range but lower energy as a trade-off.

The second risk to consider is with respect to AKY-2519 as a miniprotein radioconjugate targeting B7-H3-expressing solid tumor patients. The company is expected to present two poster presentations for this radiotherapeutic at the upcoming ASCO 2026 medical conference on May 30, 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the data presented on these posters are going to be positive, first and foremost. Secondly, even if the data presented is highly positive, there is no assurance that the stock market will react positively to it, causing the share price to trade higher. A phase 1b study for this program is expected to start in mid-2026. The risk of this is that even if positive data is achieved with AKY-1189 targeting Nectin-4-expressing solid tumor patients, there is no assurance that its counterpart targeting B7-H3-expressing solid tumor patients will have a similar or better outcome.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of competition in the solid tumor targeting space for its candidates AKY-1189 and AKY-2519. There are two aspects to consider here. First, PADCEV as an ADC is a Nectin-4-targeting therapy that has been approved to treat patients with locally advanced or metastatic urothelial cancer [mUC]. Thus, the first risk is that there is no guarantee that AKY-1189 as a miniprotein radioconjugate will be able to treat these mUC patients in a superior manner over that of PADCEV. Failure to achieve a competitive advantage, either in safety or efficacy, would be a major problem for Aktis Oncology. The second risk to consider would be the broad nature in which this company is moving its candidates, AKY-1189 and/or AKY-2519, forward. The reason why is because the goal is to expand beyond that of smaller targets like PSMA and/or SSTR2. These protein targets address 24k and 38k patients, respectively. The goal is to expand the radiopharmaceutical market to go after a larger pool of patients [Aktis Oncology Corporate Presentation, March 2026, slide #4]. Specifically, to use these candidates to go after solid tumors that express Nectin-4 and B7-H3. The risk here is that there is no assurance that this goal is attainable and that the company will end up achieving positive data with such broad expression targeting. If that is the case, then it might have to shift to other protein targets, which would be a major setback.

Conclusion

Aktis Oncology was able to complete an upsized IPO, bringing in roughly $333.5 million from it. The beauty of this is that it has plenty of cash on hand to make it through several key milestones as part of its pipeline. It is already in the process of evaluating the use of AKY-1189 in a phase 1b study targeting patients with locally advanced or metastatic Nectin-4-expressing solid tumors. Preliminary data from Part 1 of this specific phase 1b study is expected in Q1 of 2027.

Plus, it is already looking at the other broad target of going after B7-H3-expressing solid tumors with a soon-to-be-initiated phase 1b study in mid-2026. With the potential to expand the scope of the class of radiopharmaceutical therapies with its miniprotein radioconjugates using the alpha-emitting isotope actinium-225, plus several catalysts expected to happen within a 1-year period, I believe that I should start this stock off with a "Buy" rating. I believe that radiotherapeutics are a strong class of drugs, but I believe it is first important to see some additional proof-of-concept [POC] in the ongoing phase 1b study.

data.title: Joby Aviation: A Speculative Buy On eVTOL, But Execution Will Decide Everything
data.description:
Shares of Joby Aviation, Inc. (JOBY), have lost nearly 50% of their value since I marked the stock a speculative buy in October 2025. It is definitely not the kind of performance that we are looking for in a name with a Buy rating, but it also highlights the inherent risk tied to investment in eVTOL companies that have yet to get their eVTOL products certified and in serial production. In this report, I discuss whether shares of Joby provide a better de-risked entry point now.

Certification Progress And Capital Raises

In the fourth quarter, Joby made an 18-point step in its certification process in phase 4. The FAA certification process has 5 stages:

  1. Certification Basis to define rules and requirements.
  2. Means of compliance to set a framework to prove compliance.
  3. Certification plans to provide detailed testing plans.
  4. Testing & Analysis in which the actual aircraft is being tested and analyzed.
  5. Certification with Type Inspection Authorization flights.

By March 2026, the company’s first FAA-conforming aircraft took flight. This is a major milestone, as it moves the certification from prototype testing to FAA-conforming aircraft testing and generally marks a significant step in Stage 4 and readies the company to transition to the last phase of Type Inspection Authorization flights. During the TIA flight testing, FAA pilots test the aircraft, and once everything is compliant and to the satisfaction of the FAA, the airplane will be certified. Certification is expected this year. On the 27th of April, the company completed the first point-to-point air taxi flight.

Stage four is generally one of the most cash-intensive, as the company builds prototypes as well as confirming aircraft and tests both. Joby Aviation issued $600 million in convertible notes and 52.9 million shares at $11.35. I believe that capital raises are part of the game for Joby Aviation. Share prices most of the time decline sharply when a capital raise is announced, and for Joby Aviation, this was no exception. The discount on the offering was around 15%, and that was higher than the 11% discount to the pre-offering price in October 2025. So, that exerts additional pressure on share prices.

The Impact On Joby Aviation Stock Price Targets

Joby Aviation is not an easy stock to value. The company is not expected to have positive EBITDA until 2030, which I believe would bring the share price target to $16 based on an EV/EBITDA that remains elevated at three times the peer group multiple. This would indicate 76% upside and $15.24 when implementing the conversion of the convertible notes due 2032, which have an initial conversion price of $14.19. The EV/Sales method would show that currently the stock is valued with 2030 revenues in mind. At 2 times the peer group multiple, there would be around 115% upside with a $19.55 target or $19.29 after potential dilution from convertible notes, and at three times the peer group multiple, there would be 225% upside or 217% after conversion of notes. However, that third scenario is not one that I feel extremely comfortable with. On EV/Sales, I do believe that a more realistic price target would be based on Scenario 2.

If we run a price-to-sales analysis, we would get a diluted price target of $16.57. What we actually see is that valuing Joby Aviation is far from straightforward. I am currently applying a $13.87 price target based on 2029 sales in Scenario three, but that is not a multiple that I’d expect to hold. I believe that the 8% upside against 2030 sales and the fair valuation against sales in 2029 using the EV/Sales method suggest that there is a potential speculative entry point. From there, the base case price target would be $13.87, and after that, $15.24 based on an early EV/EBITDA assessment for 2030.

The complicating factor here is that as sales scale, we cannot assume the EV/Sales multiple to remain constant, and I am also inclined to switch from EV/Sales to EV/EBITDA for 2030. It probably shows how speculative the opportunity remains even for Joby Aviation as the front-runner in eVTOL commercialization. The company will have its most cash-intensive years through 2028, and while they are well capitalized, the valuation is going to be highly dependent on commercial adoption and the ramp-up in production to drive operating leverage and revenues.

Conclusion: Joby Aviation Remains A Speculative Opportunity

Joby Aviation is without a doubt the front-runner in the Western world on eVTOL development. However, we also note that while the company remains well-capitalized to carry it through the most cash-intensive years, it needs near-perfect execution on the commercial production ramp-up to prevent any further needs for capital raises. The positive likely is that if commercial demand is as firm as expected, there should be more space for Joby Aviation to raise funds through issuing debt rather than diluting shareholders. For now, I am maintaining my speculative Buy rating for Joby Aviation.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Income-Covered Closed-End Fund Report

(This was formerly the "Quality" CEF Report, now renamed to better reflect the focus on distribution coverage)

Quantitative screens help to rapidly narrow down attractive candidates from the database of ~400 closed-end funds for further due diligence and investigation.

Based on feedback from our members, we recognize that distribution coverage and return of capital (ROC) are key metrics for many investors. While ROC requires deeper analysis beyond simply viewing it as negative, many investors prefer funds with coverage above 100%, meaning distributions are fully supported by earnings. This level of coverage can suggest a lower risk of distribution cuts, which are events that often trigger significant price declines. The Income-Covered CEF Report therefore screens for funds where net investment income provides distribution coverage exceeding 100%.

However, several important considerations should be noted. Investors should always verify current coverage ratios using official fund documents before making investment decisions. Additionally, while coverage above 100% is favorable, it doesn't guarantee stable distributions, as funds may adjust their payouts as market conditions change. Finally, our 100% screening threshold, while practical for filtering purposes, remains arbitrary; there's very little meaningful difference between funds at 99.9% versus 100.1% coverage.

How is Coverage Calculated?

For the following fund houses, the latest trailing coverage numbers from the fund's latest unaudited monthly estimates are used:

  • PIMCO: trailing 6-month coverage
  • BlackRock: trailing 3-month coverage
  • Nuveen: trailing 3-month coverage
  • Eaton Vance: trailing 3-month coverage

Coverage ratios for the remaining funds are based on earnings data from their latest annual or semi-annual reports. Since these reports are published just twice yearly, these coverage figures may lag by up to 6 months compared to funds that provide monthly earnings estimates.

Commentary

A number of floating-rate CEFs make the top lists in this month's Income-Covered CEF Report, led by Eaton Vance Senior Floating-Rate Trust (EFR) which is once again the top pick from this month's DxYxZ screen. EFR currently trades at an attractive -11.49% discount, with a distribution yield of 7.79% with 104% coverage. Its -1.5 z-score indicates relative undervaluation compared to its recent 1-year average discount.

The third-ranked DxYxZ fund, Eaton Vance Floating-Rate Income Trust (EFT), also trades with attractive metrics of a -10.39% discount, 7.68% yield (101% coverage), and a -1.2 z-score.

With portfolios of senior loans, EFR and EFT's incomes are highly dependent on prevailing interest rates. As such, falling interest rates over the past two years have led to a commensurate decrease in these funds' income.

The Eaton Vance floaters employ a variable distribution policy: they adjust their distribution as portfolio income changes, month-by-month if necessary. As a result, EFR and EFT's distribution levels have closely followed the Secured Overnight Financing Rate ("SOFR").

This policy helps protect the funds' capital base and reduces reliance on return of capital distributions to fund payouts. Moreover, it also effectively eliminates the risk of "surprise" distribution cuts negatively impacting a CEF's share price if shareholders have grown accustomed to a higher, steady payout. That said, this policy may be less attractive to investors who prioritize a steady, predictable income stream, since distributions move up and down with market conditions.

What is particularly interesting is that these funds' discount narrowed as distributions rose, only to widen back out again as payouts declined. This relationship highlights the idea that many CEF investors are income-focused, often reacting more to changes in the distribution rate rather than changes in underlying portfolio value.

For the contrarian CEF investor, these kinds of premium/discount movements can create opportunity. When falling short-term rates cause senior loan distributions to decrease and yield-oriented holders to rotate out, discounts can widen beyond what NAV fundamentals would justify. In turn, this can create attractive entry points for investors willing to look through near-term distribution pressure and position for eventual discount reversion as the rate cycle turns.

data.title: RCM Technologies: Decent Performance, Keep Riding The Wave Higher (Downgrade)
data.description:
It’s been a year and a half since I looked at RCM Technologies, Inc. (RCMT), and since I sold all my positions last year before this recent spike, I wanted to see if it would be a good time to start another position again and go through its performance. In short, if you are already a shareholder in RCMT, then there is no reason to sell now. Just keep riding the momentum.

Briefly On Performance

Starting from the top, we can see the company’s revenues got a significant boost starting in 2025, with an acceleration in the back half of the year. The main drivers for this performance were Specialty Health Care and Engineering, with most of the growth coming from Engineering, which got a boost from a record backlog and demand tied to infrastructure, grid modernization, aerospace and defense, and data-center-related work.

Specialty Health Care benefited from stronger demand for staffing and related services.

For the latest financial year, 2025, we can see that Specialty Health Care continues to be the main revenue generator, which grew around 15% y/y, a respectable growth. Engineering grew around 25%, a very respectable growth rate. Life Sciences, Data, and Solutions segment disappointed us with around an 11% decline for the year, to $34.8m.

Going over the company’s profitability and efficiency, we can see that for the last three years, RCMT’s profitability took a slight hit. It never had impressive margins, and now they are even lower than before.

What we can conclude from such a performance is that the company saw growth in revenue, but that growth was not as high-value as before. In other words, the company was involved in a lot of activity that carried lower margins. Lower-margin services and scaling businesses like healthcare staffing and project work. The prior mix back in 2023 was a lot more profitable, but I think the growth in the top line more than offset the slight margin compression.

Looking at the other efficiency metrics, we can see that even if there was a slight profitability decline, the management is utilizing the company’s assets and shareholder capital rather efficiently. Additionally, its return on total capital is fantastic. One of the best ones I’ve seen in a while, which to me means I wouldn’t mind owning the company at a slightly higher premium.

ROE seems a little elevated, and that is because it is artificially inflated by the company’s financial leverage ratio, which was over 3x. This implies the company may be running high on debt, but that alone doesn’t tell us the whole situation.

So, for the year 2025, the company had around $3m in cash against $24.6m in long-term debt. This kind of supports our assumption that it may have a decent amount of leverage on its books that is inflating its ROE. I wouldn’t say it’s bad, however. Operating income for the year was $25m, while it paid out $2.6m in interest expenses, meaning its interest coverage ratio was around 9.4x, well above 2x, which many analysts say is healthy, and even passes my more conservative requirement of at least 5x. Additionally, the company had a healthy cash flow generation for the year, which grew 207% y/y to $18.96m, with free cash flow coming in at $17.3m. So, 3.3x financial leverage seems to be more than supported by such a balance sheet and cash flow generation ability. No issues.

Overall, the company seems to have performed rather well, even if its profitability took a slight hit recently. Its top-line growth more than offset any of that.

Comments On The Outlook

The weird thing is, the company’s share price took off after the Q4 report, which was a double beat, and it seems that the management didn’t hold a conference call to discuss the results in more detail, and I cannot find a reason why, besides the fact that they couldn’t file the FY25 10-K on time. The market reacted very positively to the earnings report and awarded the company with a 68% share price increase in 1 month.

The only explanation I can think of is the fact that the results were great. The company’s gross profit increased 10%, adjusted EBITDA came in around $5m higher than last year, while its adjusted net income per diluted share was also 23% higher than the same time last year. Even after this performance and a huge run-up in its share price, it is still trading at an 11.5x forward P/E ratio.

In general, I would like to see another great year for Engineering and Healthcare. Can the company keep up the momentum in these segments for 2026, or will there be some stoppers that will soften their growth?

The backlog in engineering will most likely see its momentum supported by further grid modernization, as well as the aerospace and defense sectors, which are always looking to upgrade their technology. In Q3, the management said that they believe the company has strong momentum going into 2026, and this was proven by solid growth numbers from the two main revenue generators. Engineering backlog for 2026 already surpassed $70m, compared to only $21m in 2025, so I believe this was one of the reasons the company’s share price skyrocketed.

For the upcoming quarter, it is expected that the company will do $0.70 and $0.62 in adjusted and GAAP EPS, respectively, on $90.76m in revenues. That translates to around 5% sequential growth as well as around 7% y/y.

Whether RCMT beats or misses on these estimates is hard to tell. The most recent ones were beaten, but overall, in the last ten quarters, the company disappointed.

Is It Still Worth Buying RCMT?

Let’s take a look at a quick DCF model. Below are the assumptions I used, which may not be as conservative as I usually go.

Especially on the revenue side of things. It’s hard to model anything after 2 years because there aren’t many analysts that cover the company, but I decided to taper it off slightly. The company managed to grow at around 12.5% CAGR since FY21, but to keep it a little more conservative, I am going with the above rates for the next 5 years. Margins, I am keeping close to where they are now, because they barely move at all, as we saw earlier. The rest of the model is as close to the company’s historic averages as possible. So, with these assumptions, we get the following projections.

For the DCF model, since the company’s ROTC is very attractive, I went with its WACC as my discount rate, which was around 11.6%. I usually like to add on top of the company’s WACC for some margin of safety, but I’m alright with not adding any right now. Also, I am using 2.5% as my terminal growth rate. So, with that said, we can see that RCMT’s intrinsic value is around $34.22 per share, which means it is trading close to its fair value if the assumptions above hold.

I suggest keeping your position in RCMT, especially now that it has started to show some life recently. Keep riding the wave, because I don’t see a lot of appealing options to get into right now. If the company can maintain the growth in the backlog across the business, we should see a lot of growth remaining in the company’s share price, even after this sudden increase. I wouldn’t be surprised to see some profit-taking just because it did shoot up so much and so quickly. I sold out of my position some time ago, as the share price didn’t go anywhere, and I found better use of the capital at that time, which worked out as well.

data.title: Centene: Big Comeback Story
data.description:
Make no mistake at all: Centene Corporation (CNC) has experienced a very difficult year. Why? Well, it is a health insurer, and that has been a very bad neighborhood to be in the last year. But why? Well, we have seen massive increases to insurers cost ratios. We have also seen concerns over reimbursement rates. However, we have been of the belief that last summer was the worst of it for many of these companies. We have not covered Centene stock since last summer, but the stock is breaking out now, it would appear, so it is appropriate to check in.

This earnings season has been important for many companies, and health insurers have been especially on watch. We have been looking for true confirmation of an earnings inflection in these companies. One piece of news that led the sector higher as we came into April was on the Medicare Advantage service reimbursement rates coming. They were higher than initially was thought, which helped juice the sector. Now that earnings are coming in, we are seeing the companies also take a leg higher. We did not expect much out of these stocks until later this year, but the move is welcomed. Centene is now making a comeback. It just reported earnings that significantly exceeded expectations. Let us discuss the just-reported Q1 earnings and the outlook.

Well, we actually saw Centene report revenue that was a big beat versus the consensus expectations. Folks, while revenues were expected to be around flat, they actually came in at $49.94 billion, a $2.4 billion beat against consensus. It was also up 7.1% from last year. This was a great start to the report. While revenue being up is important, really the story that should be of interest is the margins.

That said, premium and service revenues increased a solid 5% to $44.7 billion from $42.5 billion a year ago. This was better than expected. But why was it better than expected? Well, there was strong premium yield and membership growth in the Medicare Prescription Drug Plan business, as well as state-directed payments. Further, and importantly, as this was something we had surmised was coming in 2026, there were rate increases to address medical trends in the Medicaid business. So how about the margin story?

It all comes down to the cost ratio, which Centene reports as the so-called "health benefits ratio." One thing we learned in 2025 was that, for whatever reason, people were using more care, and the care has been more expensive. We saw the cost ratio surge into the mid-90% range last year and crush earnings. We still do not have a very good explanation as to why 2025 usage spiked, though there had been some higher drug costs and more usage of behavioral health claims. But that said, the combined cost was down to 87.3%. This was actually down from 87.5% last year and well off the peak we saw in 2025.

This was in part due to rate hikes and revenue increases, but also improvements in managing medical costs. And it was noted that the company saw "moderate flu costs". There was also an increase to the premium deficiency reserve they had in 2025, but no such reserve was present in this quarter for the Medicare Advantage business. This is because that line is pushing toward profitability. We should also point out cost controls on the spending for general and administrative costs. Those came down to 7.6% as a ratio to revenue versus 7.9% a year ago.

When we combine the higher revenues than expected with a lower composite cost usage ratio and lower spending on sales, general, and administrative expenses, the company put out a tremendous earnings beat, folks. The company saw GAAP income per share of $3.11 with adjusted EPS of $3.37. Folks, this was a whopping $1.24 above consensus and well above the company's internal expectations, as they noted in the release. This has helped shares rally.

The balance sheet is worth looking at here, too, given the tough year that was 2025. At the end of the quarter, the company had cash and investments of $41.8 billion and $437 million of cash available to spend. Cash flow in the quarter was $4.4 billion. This was mostly from earnings power but also the partial sale of the 2025 CMS Prescription Drug Program receivables. The company chipped down $1 billion of debt in the quarter as well.

We should note that the medical claims liabilities totaled $20.6 billion. The company's days in claims payable were 48 days, which was a 2-day increase compared to last year. Total debt was $16.4 billion. There are no borrowings on its $4.0 billion revolving credit facility.

What about as we look ahead? A lot of companies are beating Q1 earnings but are not raising guidance. We are not seeing that really with the health insurers, and certainly not with Centene. It was bullish that the company raised its estimates. The company upped its 2026 premium and service revenues guidance range by $1.0 billion. It now sees those revenues coming in at a range of $171.0 billion to $175.0 billion, driven by Medicaid revenue. Further, with the company reducing its cost ratios on top of upping its revenue guidance, it raised its EPS projection. On a GAAP basis, it sees at least $2.37. But for the year, it now sees adjusted EPS of at least $3.40. This is why shares are rallying here to $49 at the time of this writing. That puts shares at 14.4X FWD the floor of the EPS outlook. And it shows Centene is returning to growth for the year. In fact, if it hits that floor, it will be 63.4% growth from 2025. With the return to growth, shares are re-rating higher. We think CNC stock is a Buy.

data.title: I Bought 50 Undervalued Stocks In 2019 And 2020: Here's How They Performed
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier this month, I wrote an article titled "I Shared 42 Sell Ratings In 2019 - Here Is How They Have Performed Long Term." The purpose of that article was to use the data from real public "sell" articles I wrote in 2019 and early 2020 on high-quality businesses that were overvalued to demonstrate that valuation matters over the long term, and that if a person has a good valuation method, then they can improve returns by selling or trimming overvalued positions, or by simply avoiding buying overvalued stocks.

The vast majority of readers found the article compelling, but several readers in the comment section requested data on my "buys" during that time. For several reasons, initially, I didn't think an article about my purchases would be as useful or compelling as one about the "sells." The first reason is that I have led an investing group, The Cyclical Investor's Club, on Seeking Alpha since 2019, so not all of my buys were public record like the sell articles were. Second, while I did write about two-thirds of the purchases publicly, they were not all published near the time of the purchases. Third, I only purchased four stocks using this valuation method during the same timeframe for the 42 sell-rated stocks, so the sample size was too small to be useful. And, fourth, it would take some work to compile the data and organize it in a way that's useful to readers. The particular valuation method I used for the sell articles isn't the only method I use, so I would have to parse out that valuation method from others, and I would need to choose a timeframe that would be useful yet unbiased. And lastly, selling and buying are not apples-to-apples comparisons. If a stock is overvalued enough, then overvaluation can be both a necessary and sufficient reason to sell or avoid it. But just because a stock's valuation is attractive, it isn't necessarily a sufficient reason to buy it. It might be a lower-quality or declining business and therefore deserves the undervaluation.

In the end, despite my reservations that readers might not find a similar article on my buys as compelling and useful, it was made clear in the comment section that readers wanted it. So, this article is my attempt to please those readers and provide the most useful data I can, despite all the challenges I mentioned earlier.

To make the article more useful and to provide additional context to the numbers, I have included an extensive bonus section in the last half of this article where I share lots of additional information about how my individual stock strategies and portfolio strategy have been developed and evolved over time to work as a comprehensive investment strategy. My goal is to add context and value for readers so the numbers aren't taken in complete isolation.

Choosing the Best Time Frame

Over the past decade, I have developed my own investing strategies and techniques rather than using more common ones or copying others. So, I've never tried to directly copy the strategies of super investors like Warren Buffett or Ben Graham, and I've never fully adopted an investing "theme" like, say, dividend growth investing. I also never focused tightly on a particular industry, as Wall Street analysts often do. Each of my strategies has different ways of taking advantage of mispricings in the market (typically over the medium term of 2-5 years, but sometimes longer). This is usually different from most other investors, and when all the techniques are combined at the portfolio level, they create a larger strategy that's unique from any other investor or investing philosophy. This means I often need to explain what I'm doing in a more detailed manner than other investors; otherwise, readers tend to make the wrong assumptions about my goals and methods. That said, usually when I cover a stock, I focus on a single strategy applied to a single stock because it makes the thesis clearer and the article shorter. But in this case, I'm going to zoom way out and examine several strategies, in addition to the results of my stock purchases in 2019 and 2020, because how these strategies developed over time and work together is important to understand why the returns, while very good, are not as great as they appear in isolation.

The first of these unique strategies is the Deep Cyclical Strategy, which I developed in late 2015 and then started writing about publicly in early 2016. This has been a very effective strategy, and one of the most profitable for me over the years, but eventually I realized that the strategy covered a fairly narrow range of stocks. I estimate that somewhere between 100 and 200 stocks in the entire global stock market met the basic criteria for the strategy. Additionally, the times when there was a decent amount of "buys" using the strategy tended to be very lumpy. Sometimes there were a lot of opportunities, but other times, we could go two or three years with almost nothing to buy. So, soon after I started my investing group, I began researching a new strategy to help expand the universe of stocks I could cover. By April of 2019, I had developed the earnings-based "Full-Cycle Strategy," and that was the strategy I used to do the valuation analysis on those 42 stocks in my last article.

That article used a timeframe from April 2019 through February 2020, just before the market recognized the pandemic dangers and crashed. Ideally, I could go back to that same timeframe and examine a few dozen "buys" that I made using this strategy and share those results. However, I only purchased four stocks using this strategy during that time. That's a very small sample size and not very useful. Those four stocks produced positive returns, but all of them underperformed the S&P 500 while I owned them. Overall, this was in line with my general assessment that most of the market was overvalued at the time (hence all the "sell' articles). There were simply many more overvalued high-quality stocks than undervalued ones, which isn't an uncommon experience over the course of a single year, especially 10 years out from the last recession.

But it's not that the strategy never triggered buys. In the 12 months after the start of the pandemic, I made 46 purchases using the Full-Cycle Strategy. Toward the end of this period, in February 2021, I wrote my most popular article on Seeking Alpha titled "There Are Bubbles But Cash Is Not The Place To Be, Here Are 10 Stocks To Buy Now." In that article, I shared 10 stocks I thought were still good buys, and then over the course of the next year, I only wrote a single buy article. So that seems like a good date to end the time frame of this analysis since the market went through another dry spell for decent buys using that strategy.

Bringing all this together, I'm going to take the same starting date used in my previous article (April 2019), when I first started using this strategy, and end it in February 2021, the publication date of the article linked above, where I shared 10 or 11 buys in a single article. This will give a good sample size of 50 stocks to measure.

Which stocks are included?

I estimate that of these 50 stocks that were purchased, I wrote publicly on Seeking Alpha about two-thirds to four-fifths of them. However, most of those articles were not written immediately after I bought them because 1) getting real-time buys is one of the benefits of joining my investing group, and 2) I bought 30 stocks in March 2020, and there wasn't time to write an article on all of them while they were still cheap, even if I had wanted to. I did write a series about 20 of the 30 that were in the S&P 500 in late spring and early summer of 2020, though, so the purchases and valuation methods are reasonably well documented, even if they came a couple of months late. Some of these stocks I still hold but never shared publicly, but I think five or six years is now enough time to share those holdings, along with the ones I have sold, with the public.

Since the goal of my last article was to measure the results of my valuation process and whether it's useful or not to determine overvaluation, I will include only the stocks I purchased using the same strategy. All of these results come from spreadsheets accessible to members of my investing group. Typically, I use the closing prices on the day I suggest stocks are buys or the closing price the next day. These are unaudited and are mostly used by me to examine how the strategies are performing, to see if any patterns develop that might allow me to improve my techniques.

Weightings

At the time of these purchases, my standard purchase weighting was 1% of a portfolio, willing to go up to 2% in some cases. Nowadays, my standard has gotten smaller, at a .5% weighting, still maxing out at 2%. There have been a few cases where I have trimmed the initial positions by half. In order to account for the difference in weightings and make the data easier to read, I have divided the performance into four tables. The first table shares all the realized positions that I bought and then fully sold. The weightings are the standard 1% in that table. The second table contains the positions I still hold, again with 1% initial weightings each. The third table contains stocks that started with a 1% weighting, but of which I later sold half of and still hold half, or sold half of the position at different times. The tranches in this third table are .5% instead of 1%. (That difference in weighting I account for in the final table with the aggregate weighted returns.)

In each table, I compare the returns if purchased at the same time with the Invesco Equal-Weighted S&P 500 ETF (RSP) just as I did in my last article. I actually did run the numbers with SPY as well, which were about 1% higher than RSP but close to being the same, so, to make the tables simpler to read, I only included RSP here.

Here are the 50 stocks in the table below:

Gentex (GNTX), F5 Networks (FFIV), Cognizant Technology (CTSH), Prudential (PRU), Comcast (CMCSA), Bank of New York Mellon (BK), BlackRock (BK), Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), DollarTree (DLTR), Curtis Wright (CW), Ameriprise Financial (AMP), Microchip Technology (MCHP), Ozark Bank (OZK), Ametek Systems (AME), MSC Industrial (MSM), Tractor Supply (TSCO), Texas Roadhouse (TXRH), Hologic (HOLX), Candel Medical (CMD), Sysco (SYY), Rost Stores (ROST), Align Technology (ALGN), Reinsurance Group of America (RGA), UMB Financial (UMBF), Genuine Parts Company (GPC), Flir (FLIR), Autozone (AZO), PNC Financial (PNC), Eaton Vance (EV), Stryker (SYK), Arminino Foods of Distinction (AMNF), Cencora (COR), Alexion (ALXN), Intel (INTC), Ulta Beauty (ULTA), LKQ Corp (LKQ), Signature Bank (SBNY), Principal Financial (PFG), Allstate (ALL), Bristol-Meyers (BMY), People's United Financial (PBCT), PRA Health Sciences (PRAH), Elevance (ELV), NuVasive (NUVA), EBIX (EBIX), Meta (META), Lockheed Martin (LMT), T Rowe Price (TROW), Alibaba (BABA).
