The average holding period of the group has been about 21 months, or 1.75 years. Of course, these are fantastic absolute and relative returns for the full-cycle strategy during this time. They are so good, in fact, that I'm sure some readers may be skeptical. Occasionally, I get comments like "If your strategy is so good, why aren't you a billionaire?" or at least in the same spirit as that. So, in the next section, I will provide a lot more context about my strategies so readers can understand the bigger, more realistic picture.

The main question I was exploring in my first article was whether valuation matters long-term and whether valuation is possible to estimate so investors can avoid losses by taking profits or avoid overpaying. The main question here is whether understanding earnings valuations can help investors buy stocks when they're undervalued over the medium and long term. I think it's clear that having a good investing process can identify good businesses when they're undervalued and produce returns that are better than the market, as long as those buying opportunities occur. In 2020, they occurred.

After 2020, there were fewer quality opportunities, and I only added about 32 additional stocks using the Full Cycle Strategy in the following five years. If I combine all the historical returns of all the full-cycle stocks I've purchased, including those I still hold, the average return is about +35% (much lower than the +89% return of the 2020 stocks), and the average holding period is around 19 months. The estimated CAGR for the whole group is about +20.5%. This is obviously still a great return for the strategy, but much lower than the +44% CAGR achieved from the 2020 stocks.

Hopefully, this first half of the article has sufficiently answered the questions readers of my previous article had regarding how my purchases during that period performed. Next, for those who are interested in the bigger picture of how all my strategies have been developed and performed over time, I have provided a lot of additional history and context about why I invest the way that I do, and how these strategies and techniques came to be.

Bonus Portfolio Strategy Context

In this section, there are four levels of examination and history. First, I share some of the core principles I adopted over a decade ago that I wanted to uphold as an analyst. Next, I share the history of how my four main investing strategies developed over time. Then I will share the overall performance compared to what I think is the best portfolio benchmark. And lastly, I'll highlight some of the challenges of the big picture investing strategy going forward.

Early Principles

Soon after I started writing at Seeking Alpha in late 2015, I adopted a few principles I wanted to uphold that would later carry over to my investing group in 2019. Some of those core principles were:

  1. I would only have "Buy" ratings on stocks I actually bought myself, which meant that I was incentivized to get good returns rather than just article clicks.
  2. I would only buy stocks when they were good values and had a margin of safety. There were no stocks that were perpetual buys all the time because I thought the businesses were so great that it was worth paying any price. (It's fairly rare for me to have more than five buy ratings at any given time in the entire market.)
  3. I would measure the results of my strategies to the best of my ability and openly share them when possible.
  4. I would account for cash, or where money was when not invested in individual stocks, because in the real world, investors don't have magic piles of cash sitting around to "buy more" of falling stocks.
  5. I wouldn't regularly first buy a stock in my investing group, and then later promote it as a "buy" to the public at a higher price or valuation.

These basic principles, along with a few others, would color how my strategies would develop over time.

Phase 1: Pre-Pandemic

In 2019, when I started my investing group, I only had a single strategy, the Deep Cyclical Strategy, which I had been using and writing about on Seeking Alpha for about three full years. Over the course of that first year, I decided to use this strategy in conjunction with index investing. It was meant to work as a simple way to boost returns of some basic index strategies (for example, I liked SPLV and RSP at the time as the default indexes). Essentially, investors could add individual Deep Cyclical stocks when they were cheap and then take profits when they reached their return goals, and those returns would boost the returns of the index funds. In short, the idea was to be fully invested all the time in S&P 500-like indexes and select individual cyclical stocks when they were very good deals.

By early 2019, I knew that I wanted to cover a larger universe of stocks, so I developed the Full-Cycle Strategy, which is the strategy we reviewed in this article.

In retrospect, my returns (so far) would have been better had I kept to that original plan of using my strategies to supplement index investing. But I understood the dangers of the pandemic relatively early compared to most investors, and by the end of February 2020, I didn't think it was wise or responsible to be 100% in stocks with my family's money. And if that was the case, then I felt the same way for followers of my work. So on 2/28/20 I took the ETF "default" money and moved it to cash instead. I published a public blog post on SA the next day, on 2/29/20, where I explained what I had done. The blog was titled "Recession Mode Has Arrived."

Phase 2: Early Pandemic

As stock prices fell in March 2020, I used the two strategies I had at the time, along with some precious metals purchases, and started buying stocks in 1% weightings. In addition to the 50 Full Cycle stocks I shared earlier, I also bought 27 Deep Cyclical stocks during that same time period as well. The Deep Cyclicals averaged a +48% return compared to SPY's +37% return over the same time periods. So, both strategies performed extremely well in the year following my February 2020 cash raise.

I also developed a new REIT strategy in mid-2020. It's not a strategy I share publicly, and it never exceeded 10% of portfolio weighting. It's a solid strategy, but we haven't had the best environment for medium-term REIT investing lately. I've had about 17 REIT positions over the past six years, and they only returned an average +8.6%. It's good they have positive returns, but they have dragged my overall average returns down a bit.

Phase 3: Late Pandemic

As time went on through 2021, I found myself consistently holding "too much" cash, especially considering the federal government was starting to push a couple of trillion dollars of stimulus into the economy. By this time, I knew the strategies I had developed up until that point were excellent strategies, but they were getting turned over quickly in the volatile market because stocks were quickly going from significantly undervalued to overvalued. One of the most difficult challenges I've had is government and central bank rescue packages and the lack of a real recession since 2009. This creates a situation with higher than average valuations, higher inflation, and a "buy the dip" mentality with more and more investors. In a world with less government borrowing and spending, I would have much less turnover in the portfolio, but there hasn't been a reduction of this pattern for more than a decade. Since I have a hard time allowing myself to buy stocks I know aren't good deals or to hold stocks I think are very overvalued, and with the strategies I was using with great success, I set out to develop a new buy-and-hold growth stock strategy that was designed to ignore high valuations once the purchase had been made. In theory, over time, this would mean that about half of my portfolio positions would be buy and hold, and half would be cyclicals or full cycle stocks sensitive to overvaluation and so would likely turn over every two to five years.

I knew that amongst the hundreds of SPACs and IPOs that had gone public during the pandemic, most would be massive losers, but that, eventually, in the rubble of a future decline, I might be able to find some early-stage long-term growth businesses at reasonable prices.

That expectation of an IPO crash was correct:

I had learned from research performed at the Arizona State University School of Business (along with personal observation of companies like Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOGL), and Netflix (NFLX)) that the majority of long-term stock market returns historically have come from a handful of huge winners and that sometimes those companies could be purchased at good prices after they were public. So, I set forth to develop a buy-and-hold long-term growth stock strategy to combine with my other strategies. The new strategy, if I could figure it out, would reduce the turnover in my portfolio because it was buy and hold, and could potentially capture more long-term huge winners I might otherwise miss because of the tight parameters of my other strategies. The trade-off is that the growth strategy would naturally be more volatile and have a higher failure rate (which was generally only about 20% with my other strategies).

It took me about a year's worth of research and work before I launched the new strategy, which I call the Profit Growth Strategy, in September of 2021.

The initial results of the strategy were, to put it mildly, very bad. There are 20 of these positions that have been realized (which included a disproportionate number of the initial purchases that didn't work out well). The average return of these realized positions was about -33%. But every strategy I've developed over the years usually has some flaws that are exposed in the first six months to a year of actual practice, and there are always some adjustments that can be made once the issues are identified. For that reason, I didn't give up on the strategy. I currently have 25.5 positions that I'm still holding from this strategy, and the average return of those positions is +34%. So, things are now on the right track, but I certainly took a hit to my overall returns while getting the strategy dialed in, and that brought down the average portfolio returns.

Current Phase

Putting all this together, since I started the Cyclical Investor's Club on 1/12/2019, the total returns, including cash drag, occasional ETF purchases (mostly precious metal ETFs and a few country ETFs), and all of my combined strategies, have been about +120% as of mid-April. That's about an +11.50% CAGR. Over the past seven-plus years, my cash levels have been as high as 70% at the start of the pandemic to near zero in 2019 and the end of 2021, but cash has averaged around 25% to 30% over time, and has been 30% to 40% since mid-2022. So, I've averaged holding quite a bit of cash over the years, which becomes a drag on performance when the market is at all-time highs, as it is right now.

While I still make occasional tweaks to my individual strategies when I identify weaknesses or opportunities, the core four strategies now cover a wide universe of stocks and complement one another well at the portfolio level. So for the past three years or so, I've only made adjustments around the edges. Additionally, now that I've been doing this a while, I've had time to understand that my personal inclination is to be prepared for downturns and focus on the medium and long-term time frames. I hate having to adjust my portfolio much based on news, black swans, or even quarterly earnings reports. And I like having cash to confidently buy stocks during sell-offs, crashes, and recessions. I've come to understand that's a part of my personality that is unlikely to change, and so I have accepted that and have a big picture investing strategy that is in line with it.

Because of these predispositions of aiming for great returns while being prepared for downturns (one might call it an anti-fragile growth strategy), I have adopted a two-pronged, long-term investing portfolio strategy approach that matches my instincts and tendencies. The first goal of that strategy is to achieve a long-term return (20 to 30-year time frame across multiple cycles) of an 8% CAGR plus inflation.

Annualized US Inflation since 2019 has been +3.82%, so my return goals would roughly be 8% + 3.82%, or +11.82%. My actual returns have been right in line with those goals.

It's worth noting that these returns have been easy for the wider market to meet during the past 10 to 15 years but have only been achieved long-term by the very best investors.

Over the past 30 years, for example, Berkshire Hathaway has achieved an 8.02% CAGR plus inflation. Since 2008, the S&P 500 has perpetually underperformed that metric.

Importantly, these goals are independent of whatever the indices might be doing over the short term. Usually, if there's a big boom, it's eventually followed by a big bust. Which brings me to my second goal:

I want to avoid spending more than 12 quarters deeper than -40% off my portfolio's inflation-adjusted high.

Again, this can sound like a relatively easy goal to achieve, but it's worth noting that neither the S&P 500 nor the Nasdaq 100 has avoided it during my lifetime.

From 2000 to 2015, QQQ spent 14 years -40% or more below its inflation-adjusted high.

I don't have data that includes dividends, but the S&P 500 spent from mid-1975 until 1986 more than -40% off its inflation-adjusted high.

So, right now, with the market at all-time highs, much like the late 1960s or the late 1990s, my two goals of achieving an 8% plus inflation long-term CAGR and avoiding an extended drawdown of -40% more than three years off the inflation-adjusted high might seem quaint. In the context of history, though, they aren't easy long-term goals.

Current Challenges

One of the fundamental assumptions I make with all of my strategies is that history is a good guide to the future. Generally speaking, as long as I control for a handful of factors, about 80% of the time or more, a business will perform similarly to how it has in the past. Because of this, I weigh past patterns heavily in my individual stock analyses.

Rationally, this approach makes a lot of sense. While nothing guarantees future results (as the saying goes), historic statistics and results do matter. For example, all things being equal, no rational baseball manager would fill a team roster with players who have a historical .200 batting average if they could instead have a team with a history of batting .300. Is it possible the team that previously averaged .200 improves enough to end the next season with a better record than the team that averaged .300? Sure, it's possible, but very unlikely. History is the best indicator of what is likely to happen in the future.

That said, I'm diligent about not ignoring "Sea Change" moments, as Howard Marks recently suggested we might be in. Currently, we have three potential sea changes (which is a lot). The first is that we may have consistently rising interest rates and inflation for the first time in decades. The stock market, valuation-wise, is still priced for very low interest rates. At the beginning of 2007, the S&P 500 had an average P/E ratio of around 17. That was near the peak of the last bull market. Now, after many years of low interest rates, government stimulus, and tax cuts, the P/E ratio is over 25. That's about a 50% higher valuation. If interest rates rise, government stimulus is reduced, and there are no more tax cuts (or perhaps even tax raises for corporations), there could be a massive reduction in the price of stocks. Most of this hinges on interest rates, and consistently rising rates are not something investors have experienced for a long time.

The second potential sea change is deglobalization and geopolitics. The world is rapidly moving away from a unipolar world to a multipolar one. Whatever else happens, it will be a less efficient world, and it will also be more difficult for huge businesses to scale globally. A handful of these huge businesses now make up around 40% of the major stock indexes. Layer wars on top of all this, and the situation gets worse. The globalized world in which economic profit is always more important than strategic safety could change. Additionally, we have a US administration that some feel is challenging in terms of following civilizational norms. Norms, almost by definition, are guided by historical behavior, practices, and policies. If those things go out the window, then it naturally reduces history's predictive power.

And third, we have AI and whatever changes it might bring. Not only economically, but also socially. This could rapidly disrupt previously solid businesses, and we already see some of this happening in software stocks and the rapid acquisition of electric power resources.

To go back to my baseball analogy, these potential sea changes could fundamentally change the rules of the game. It may be like if baseball changed to allow steroid use, added a salary cap, and had some of the best players randomly drafted into military service over the course of the next few years. If those things were to happen, it wouldn't negate the usefulness of players' historical statistics when building a team, but it wouldn't make the stats as useful because so many things changed simultaneously at the player and team level.

Returning to investing, while it's true that most of the coming changes make predicting the future more difficult based on the past, one thing that helps make it a little easier is that the majority of these changes and potential changes are negative for productivity and the economy. The only exception is a literal handful of AI companies, which are very difficult to value medium term, even if they're successful at making other industries more productive for a while. Everything else is pretty negative for business and the business climate. So, that makes it easier to hold a good amount of cash for a while until there is more clarity, or valuations come down. It also makes sense to stay diversified even if it is difficult to find great deals in this market.

For the past three years or so, my approach has been mostly a barbell of diversified growth stocks and occasional deep cyclical stocks on one end and floating rate treasuries (TFLO) on the other end. Right now, about 40% of the portfolio is in TFLO, and each week I buy at least one .5% weighted stock position, which keeps me actively looking hard for opportunities. This approach helps keep up with rising inflation via the growth stocks while still having cash to deploy if the fragile stagflation we are in cracks at some point.

My stock portfolio roughly contains about 40% international stocks outside the US and 40% that are less than a $10 billion market cap across multiple industries. I don't have set allocations I'm aiming for, but I will not exceed 50% cash/treasuries. Over time, my overall approach has evolved into trying to get bull market returns in line with an equal-weighted S&P 500 ETF while taking on the lower risk of a traditional 60/40 allocation. Probably the best benchmark right now is Vanguard's Balanced Fund (VBINX). Since mid 2020, when I added cash and grew my individual stock selections, I have been logging returns on a weekly basis relative to a variety of indexes, as well as my 8% CAGR plus inflation goal. Here is how they have done.

The blue line is my approximate portfolio performance, the green line is 8% plus inflation, and the yellow is the 60/40. I use this to get feedback on how the portfolio is positioned relative to my goals. It helps me avoid getting FOMO and chasing speculative short-term trades and keeps me focused on the big picture. It also gives me an idea of whether I may be holding too much cash.

Overall, I've consistently met my bull market return goals while outperforming a 60/40, so I don't expect any major adjustments to the portfolio strategy in the near term.

Conclusion

While investing in today's market is more challenging than ever before, having core processes that are reasonably repeatable is necessary to increase one's odds of achieving good real-world returns. I tend to be in constant pursuit of excellence (whether I like it or not), so I was never totally satisfied with rating stocks in isolation. They only really matter and make sense in the context of how they fit into a bigger portfolio strategy. Generally speaking, this industry is structurally divided into analysts and strategists. But practically speaking, these roles are inseparable. One's portfolio strategy defines 50% of whether most stocks are buys or sells at any given time. While I think I intuitively understood this early on, I'm more militant about it now. So, when readers asked for what appeared to be a simple request about the returns of my "Buys," I felt obligated to give more context so they understand that the 20%-plus long-term CAGRs of my two longest-running strategies didn't happen in isolation. Once an investor knows what they are doing, it's not that difficult to outperform the major indexes over the medium term when it comes to individual stock picks. It's much more difficult to manage everything at the portfolio level over the very long term. We will have another deep bear market someday. History has shown us this over and over again. So, I have chosen to take the alpha from these stock investments and make my portfolio more resilient by diversifying and also holding cash. The drawback of this is that as the bull market goes on, there is a drag on relative portfolio performance. The benefit is that I stay level headed in scary times and sleep well at night.

Thanks for reading. I would be happy to answer any questions in the comment section.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2 Infrastructure Plays To Power The AI Boom
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

Power is essential to everyday human life, which has never been more important than now in the digital age and, particularly, the rise of artificial intelligence. It requires massive amounts of energy to power the AI infrastructure, more specifically, the data centers that are the 'brains' behind making it all possible. Of course, with no power, that brain becomes completely useless. That is why we've seen a significant rise in the growth trajectory of infrastructure names that can provide power and/or deliver power.

Today, we are looking at two names that provide AI with the essential power but, even without AI, still remain key infrastructure plays over the long term. It is only that the AI boom has been an added catalyst. Further, these are reasonably valued plays, not egregiously overpriced, where they would expect to see a significant collapse whenever the next AI slowdown spook may occur.

#1 DTE Energy (DTE)

Starting off with perhaps a more boring potential investment that investors could consider, it is a somewhat traditional multi-utility company. They operate a distribution network of electricity and gas throughout Michigan. That said, the electric business is by far their largest segment when it comes to earnings, accounting for over 80% of their operating earnings.

They recently announced a deal with Alphabet's (GOOG)(GOOGL) Google to develop 1 GW for their data center, highlighting the potential for keeping earnings growth healthy. Analysts estimate that earnings growth over the coming years should come in between 5% and 9%. EPS growth estimates from DTE themselves show that they are expected to grow 6-8% through 2030.

Shares of DTE have been on a fairly healthy trajectory higher over the last couple of years, which has resulted in the valuations not being quite as tempting as they once were. That said, despite the expectation for healthy growth going forward, they are trading right around their historical P/E.

However, this also suggests that DTE isn't overvalued either. In fact, analysts have an average price target of near $160 for shares of DTE. According to a Morningstar analyst covering this name, they've also recently lifted their fair value estimate to $157. This was fairly meaningful because only a month prior, they had a fair value estimate of $139. The move was driven by the recent Google data center announcement, as well as a rate increase that was required and approved on February 19.

Finally, DTE can be worth owning over the long term because of its healthy and growing dividend. It comes with a dividend yield of 3.15%, which is already quite respectable; however, the 16 years of dividend growth are impressive. Even when they weren't growing their dividend in decades past, they held it consistent for many years.

In 2021, it may look like they reduced their payout, but that was to adjust for their DT Midstream (DTM) spin-off—which has done incredibly well on its own, up over 240% in only a handful of years since.

With that, DTE has seen the last decade with a CAGR of ~6.3%. Given the growing earnings and historical commitment to the dividend, that is only expected to continue going forward. That's a level that generally outpaces inflation in most years, which means it is helping to keep purchasing power growing for investors taking cash or compounding even faster for those that reinvest.

#2 Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. (BEP)

Looking at a more broad-based infrastructure play rather than a more specifically focused on a relatively geographically specific utility play like DTE, we have BEP. They provide diversified global exposure, partnering with many different companies. Their largest earnings segment comes in the form of hydro at 44%, but they also have solar, wind, and nuclear assets.

This is a limited partnership, which does mean it comes with a K-1 form at tax time. I don't personally find that to be an issue, but some investors may then prefer the alternative, Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC) shares rather than the BEP units. That can avoid the K-1 filing but retains the exact same economics and is exchangeable at the shareholder's option.

One of the downsides of BEPC shares is that you are paying a premium, which has become a bit more substantial in the last year or so. This is evident in the form of looking at the distribution yield of each. BEP's distribution yield is meaningfully higher compared to BEPC's dividend yield, despite the exact same dividend. This is specifically because BEPC's shares trade at a higher price.

At the same time, the counterargument could be made that it is always going to trade at a premium, so if we are looking at relative valuation historically, it isn't that big of a deal. I think that is a valid argument and has some merit, but it will be up to each investor to decide.

In the case of BEP, we have seen its unit price start to rise quite materially from where it was around a year ago. I know it isn't as great a bargain as it was at one time, and one could be more patient to wait for the next pullback here. Personally, I got in around $20 and then added again around $25. Units can be quite volatile, so being more patient to add more aggressively could be warranted.

Still, I think that, relatively speaking, we aren't seeing BEP at any sort of extreme valuation either. That can be backed up a bit by its historical P/FFO and where it is currently trading.

Additionally, while that is today's valuation, earnings here are expected to grow going forward at around ~9% on average in the next three years, according to analysts. BEP itself is targeting 10% FFO per unit growth. This is helped along by things like the $10 billion renewable energy deal signed with Microsoft (MSFT) last year to power its data center. Therefore, what may look like fair value or getting close to its current value may look cheap if an investor has a longer-term outlook of just 1-3 years.

Further, investors are being rewarded with a healthy distribution yield, of over 4.3%—but that's not all; it is set to continue to grow annually going forward.

They are targeting 5-9% growth going forward. This is something they've been able to achieve going back over the last decade; now there is consistent growth in their payout to investors. Over the last 5- and 10-year periods, delivering right around 5.2% annualized growth. To be fair, that is on the low end of what they are targeting going forward. However, I believe that it is still attractive for something that is already relatively higher yielding.
